title: How a Compiler Works
description: Compilers translate high-level source code into low-level machine instructions or executables before running them.
codeBlock:
Source Code
  ↓ [Lexical Analysis (Lexer)] -> Tokens
  ↓ [Syntax Analysis (Parser)] -> AST
  ↓ [Semantic Analysis] -> Checked AST
  ↓ [Optimization] -> Optimized AST
  ↓ [Code Generation] -> Target Machine Code
details:
A compiler is a complex translator that maps human-readable source code to target machine instructions (such as x86_64, ARM assembly, or web assembly) or an intermediate format. The classic compiler design splits the translation process into three decoupled sections: the Front-end, the Middle-end, and the Back-end. This separation allows compiler engineers to reuse middle-end optimizations and back-end code generators across multiple front-ends (e.g., C, Rust, Swift).

Compiler Architecture Breakdown

  • Front-end: Lexical analysis groups character streams into tokens. Syntax analysis (parsing) checks structural rules, producing an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). Semantic analysis runs type-checking, ensures scoping rules are correct, and verifies type constraints.
  • Middle-end: Converts the checked AST into a target-independent Intermediate Representation (IR), often in Static Single Assignment (SSA) form where every variable is assigned exactly once. It applies aggressive optimizations like loop unrolling, dead code elimination, and constant propagation.
  • Back-end: Handles instruction selection (mapping IR nodes to hardware-specific assembly instructions), register allocation (assigning variables to physical registers via graph coloring algorithms), and instruction scheduling to avoid pipeline stalls.

Academic References & Official Specs

  • Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools (Dragon Book) — The definitive book on parsing, type checking, and code generation theory.
  • LLVM Compiler Infrastructure Project — The industry standard modular compiler framework powering rustc, clang, and swiftc.
  • Engineering a Compiler (Cooper & Torczon) — Practical compiler design focusing on intermediate representations and code generation optimizations.

For an interactive visual walkthrough showing how code flows through these stages step-by-step, see the Interactive Compiler Pipeline Guide.
